Statements of Economic I nteres! 
322~6444 
Thank you for your letter dated (1ay 16, 1983, confirming our 
telepnone conversation concernlng the raft trips that Friends of 
tne River may provide to legislative officials. 
You 
lnfor~ea me auring our telephone conversation that Friends 
of the River, which is a loooyist employer, conducts river raft 
trlps for the purpose of providing information to tne puolic aoout 
river issues and charges the participants in these raft trips only 
the actual costs it lncurs. 
Your inquiry presented the following questions: 
(1) What disclosure and disquallfication responsibilities are 
incurred by a legislator who receives a free trip, or a discount on 
tne cost of a trip? 
(2) Is a free trip, or a discount on the cost of a trip, 
provlaea to a legislator's family memoer a gift to the legislator? 
(3) Is a gift to a legislator's staff memoer a gift to the 
legislator? 
(4) What reporting obligations would be incurred by Friends of 
tne Rlver ln connectlon with providing tne trips to leglslators, 
~nelr staffs and tnelr families? 
Following is a summary of our discussion of eacn of tne 
precedlng questions: 


Kevin Wolf 
page 2 
(I) Legislative officials are required to disclose on their 
annual Statements of Economic Interests gifts with an aggregate 
value of $50 or more 1n a year that they receive from a single 
source. 
If a single source, such as Friends of the River, provides 
a legislator with $250 or more in income, including gifts, in a 
12-month period the legislator is sUbJect co tne disqualification 
provisions of the Political Reform Act. 
The d1squalification provisions provide that: 
~NO PUblic official at any level of state or local government 
shall make, parcicipate 1n maKing or in any way attempt to use 
his official position to influence a governmental decision in 
Wh1Ch he knows or has reason to Know he has a financial 
interest." 
An official nas a "financial interest" witnin the meaning of 
Gov. Code Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeaole that the 
deciSIon will have a material financial effect on: 
"Any source of 
income ... received by or promised to the pUblic official within 12 
monchs prior to the time the decision is made." (Gov. Code Section 
d71U3 (c». 
Therefore, if a legislator receives a gift from Friends of the 
RIver witn an aggregate value of $250 or more in a 12-montn 
period,* Friends of tne River will become a "source of income" to 
the off1cial and tne officlal may nave to disqualify himself or 
nerself on decisions affecting Friends of the River. 
(2) A gift of a tr1p, or a discount on the price of a trip, 
made directly to a memoer of the legislator's immediate family is 
considered to De a gift to the family member, and not a gift to tne 
legislator. 
However, such a gift, or a discount, would be a gift 
to the leg1slator if tne legislator exercises any direction or 
control over the gift (i.e., if a family member can't go and the 
legislator chooses someone else to attend). 
(3) If a gift of a trip, or a discount on a trip, is made 
directly to a legislator's staff memoer, the trip is noc a gift to 
the legislator. 
However, such a gift, or a discount, would be a 
glft to the legislator if the legislacor exercises any direction or 
control over whO receives the free trip (l.e., if the legislator is 
allowed to bring any guest and he or she cnooses a staff memoer) . 
* The value of tne trIp 1S the fair marKet value, which is 
determIned oy what Friends of the River charges others for the 
t.r ip . 


Kevin Wolf 
page 3 
(4) As a loobyist employer, Friends of the River must report 
the value of any glfts provlded to legislative officials. 
Therefore, if the river trips are provided to legislative officials 
at no charge, or at a reduced charge, a gift has been provided and 
must be reported on Friends of the River's "Report of Lobbyist 
Employer" (Form 650). 
Such gifts are reported under Part. II, 
Sectlon A ("Activity Expenses Incurred by the Filer"). 
Furthermore, Friends of the River's lobbyist may not make or 
arrange for tne making of any gift which exceeds $10 in value to a 
legislative official. 
(See my letter dated April 7, 1983, to Corey 
Brown, Legislative Director for Friends of the River.) 
However, if tne legislatlve officlals pay the falr marKet 
value for the river trips, no disclosure is required by the 
lObbY1St employer, and no possioility for aisqualification exists 
for tne legislator. 
I nope this adequately responds to your inquiry. 
Please call 
me if you have any addltiona~ questions. 
Sincerely, 
Jeanne pritchard 
Political Reform Consultant

442-3155 
RIVER 
May 16, 1983 
Ms. Jeanne Pritchard 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, California 95804 
Dear Jeanne: 
This letter is to,confirm our conversation earlier this month regarding 
raft trips with Legislators and their staffs. 
As you know, I am currently e~ployed with Friends of the River organizing 
raft trips with reporters, celebreties, and other people. 
These people usually 
pay an amount necessary to cover our costs for food, shuttle, reservoir tows, 
and other expenses. 
Neither I nor Friends of the River have commercial permits 
on the rivers we run and therefore cannot charge more than our costs for the 
trips without violating the commercial permit system. 
We would like to expand this 
and their staffs on these trips. 
represents the law regarding raft 
~rogram to include Legislators, their families 
Please let me know if the following accurately 
trips with Legislators: 
1) 
The value of the trip is to be determined by what we charge others on 
the trip; not the price a commercial rafting company would charge. 
2) 
Any amount below that value that we charge a Legislator for the trip 
is to be considered as a gift. 
Legislators are requi red to report 
gifts over $50.00 and in no event accept an aggregate of gifts from 
one organization in a single year of more than $250.00. 
3) 
Any gifts to t:1C 
~arJJily members of a Legislator are to be included in 
the calculation of value of gifts to that Legislator. 
However, 
gifts to the staff members of the Legislator are not included in that 
calculation. 
For example, assume that a Senator, their spouse, two 
chi ldren and a staff member joined us on a two-day river trip on the 
Middle Fork of the American River without charge whi Ie others parti-
pating in the trip eash paid $35.00. 
The Senator would then have to 
report a total gift from Friends of the River of $140.00 (four trips 
x $35.00 each = $140 00). 
would also appreciate your informing me of any disclosure requirements 
we would ha 
to follow so that we could fi Ie the ap?ropriate statements as we 
lement this prog 
I look forward to hearing from you soon as the rive 
rafting season is quickly 
approaching and we are anxious to organize some trips. 
Thank you for your time and attention. 
OFFICES U)C4 TED IN 
SA CRAMFV TO 
\<4 V fRA VClSCO 


Ms. Jeanne Pritchard 
May 16, 1983

Re: 
Advice No. A-83-160 
Mary Ann Kvasager has referred your letter dated June 3, 
1983, to the Legal Division for reply. 
You asked about the 
proper reporting of a loan transaction in the primary election 
for the Los Angeles City Council. 
However your letter does not 
provide sufficiently detailed information for us to advise you 
concerning your filing obligations. 
If you will send me a 
letter containing a more specific description of the loan 
transaction in question, and of your role in this transaction, 
we will be happy to give you prompt written advice pursuant to 
Government Code Section 83114. 
If you have any questions, 
please feel free to call me at (916) 322-5901. 
Very truly yours,

sections.question: Your inquiry presented the following questions: (1) What disclosure and disqualification responsibilities are incurred by a legislator who receives a free trip, or a discount on the cost of a trip? (2) Is a free trip, or a discount on the cost of a trip, provided to a legislator's family member a gift to the legislator? (3) Is a gift to a legislator's staff member a gift to the legislator? (4) What reporting obligations would be incurred by Friends of the River in connection with providing the trips to legislators, their staffs and their families?
sections.conclusion: Legislative officials must disclose gifts of $50+ aggregate value annually from a single source. If a legislator receives $250+ from a single source in 12 months, disqualification provisions apply. Gifts to family members are not gifts to the legislator unless the legislator exercises control. Gifts to staff are not gifts to the legislator unless the legislator exercises control. As a lobbyist employer, Friends of the River must report the value of gifts provided to legislative officials on Form 650 (Report of Lobbyist Employer). If legislators pay fair market value, no disclosure is required and no disqualification applies.
sections.question_synthetic: What are the disclosure, disqualification, and reporting requirements under California law when a nonprofit lobbyist employer provides free or discounted river raft trips to legislators, their families, and staff members?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Friends of the River must report any free or discounted river trips as gifts on their Form 650, and legislators must disclose gifts exceeding $50, with a $250 annual limit from a single source before disqualification provisions apply. Gifts to family members count toward the legislator's limit if the legislator exercises control, while staff gifts do not. If legislators pay full fair market value, no reporting or disqualification requirements exist.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter from Kevin Wolf (Friends of the River) dated May 16, 1983, and the FPPC response letter from Jeanne Pritchard dated June 27, 1983. A third unrelated advice letter to Bernard S. Weiss (A-83-160) is included at the end but appears to be a different matter. Extraction focused on the primary advice regarding raft trips. High confidence despite OCR artifacts in original document.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Your inquiry presented the following questions: (1) What disclosure and disqualification responsibilities are incurred by a legislator who receives a free trip, or a discount on the cost of a trip? (2) Is a free trip, or a discount on the cost of a trip, provided to a legislator's family member a gift to the legislator? (3) Is a gift to a legislator's staff member a gift to the legislator? (4) What reporting obligations would be incurred by Friends of the River in connection with providing the trips to legislators, their staffs and their families?

CONCLUSION: Legislative officials must disclose gifts of $50+ aggregate value annually from a single source. If a legislator receives $250+ from a single source in 12 months, disqualification provisions apply. Gifts to family members are not gifts to the legislator unless the legislator exercises control. Gifts to staff are not gifts to the legislator unless the legislator exercises control. As a lobbyist employer, Friends of the River must report the value of gifts provided to legislative officials on Form 650 (Report of Lobbyist Employer). If legislators pay fair market value, no disclosure is required and no disqualification applies.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to Friends of the River's questions about providing river raft trips to California legislators, their families, and staff, addressing disclosure, disqualification, and reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act. The letter clarifies that free or discounted trips constitute gifts requiring reporting and that aggregate gift values of $250+ from a single source trigger disqualification provisions for legislators.
